lens.included_activities: Independent LMM operators of coal-, petroleum-, or natural-gas-fired generation facilities that repeatedly sell electricity, capacity, ancillary services, or contracted output to external utilities, markets, cooperatives, or large customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Vertically integrated captive generation, municipal and cooperative internal units, passive project-finance vehicles without operations, shells, transmission and distribution, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring wholesale market, power-purchase agreement, capacity, tolling, ancillary-service, or regulated generation revenue from dispatchable physical plants.

a.bridge: BLS measures power-plant operators across generation technologies and the broader electric utility sector, not six-digit fossil-only wage-weighted tasks. I map dispatch analysis, outage planning, maintenance diagnostics, compliance reporting, fuel and market forecasting, document search, and administrative workflows as exposed; control-room operation, field rounds, maintenance execution, safety response, environmental sampling, and accountable dispatch are less substitutable. The proxy may overstate exposure in small plants with lean, operations-heavy staffing.
a.rationale: AI can augment planning, diagnostics, documentation, and commercial decisions, but reliable round-the-clock physical operation remains dominant.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories from 10-15 independent gas, coal, and oil plants, including current control-system and analytics automation, would materially revise the estimate.
caveats: Public occupation data combine generation technologies and network functions.
Coal and modern gas plants have different staffing intensity.

rho.rationale: Predictive maintenance, document and alarm support, fuel and market analytics, outage planning, and compliance assistance can be implemented within five years. Cybersecurity, NERC-style operational controls, safety, legacy systems, sparse failure data, union work rules, and reliability accountability constrain deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Plant pilots measuring avoided labor hours, false alarms, forced-outage rates, maintenance cost, heat rate, and compliance review burden would move the range.
caveats: Bounded judgment rather than observed industry implementation.

e.rationale: Many establishments are held inside vertically integrated utilities, cooperatives, municipalities, large merchant portfolios, or project entities that lack transferable standalone operations. Independent contracted plants with staff, permits, interconnection, and external recurring sales fit the lens.
e.change_evidence: Screen the injected 77 candidates using EIA-860 ownership, FERC market-based-rate records, state utility filings, PPAs, operating staff, permits, and audited EBITDA.
caveats: The injected count is margin-bridged and estimated.
Plants, establishments, project entities, and transferable firms are not equivalent.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence covers small businesses generally, not power-plant transfers. I discount for portfolio ownership, passive project vehicles, regulatory approvals, debt and tax structures, environmental liabilities, PPA assignment, and internal restructurings. Merchant-plant and portfolio sales can raise transaction incidence, but many do not represent eligible standalone LMM control transfers.
s5.rationale: Energy assets trade, but qualifying firm transfers are less frequent than asset or portfolio restructurings and face extensive regulatory and environmental diligence.
s5.change_evidence: EIA ownership histories, FERC Section 203 orders, S&P Global power-plant transactions, PPA assignments, retirements, and project-finance restructurings since 2018 would establish a denominator-based rate.
caveats: Economy-wide demographic proxy is weak for capital assets.
Asset sales and qualifying operating-company transfers must be separated.

q.rationale: Competitive wholesale dispatch and capacity auctions tend to pass lower operating costs into bids and market clearing over time, while cost-of-service regulation and tolling contracts can explicitly share or return savings. Fixed-price PPAs, scarcity rents, and proprietary operating improvements preserve a portion.
q.change_evidence: Plant PPAs, tolling terms, fuel clauses, capacity contracts, market bidding data, and state rate-case treatment of operating savings would directly estimate pass-through.
caveats: Commercial structures vary from merchant to regulated and contracted plants.
No public contract sample directly measures AI-benefit retention.

d5.bridge: EIA's July 2026 outlook forecasts coal-fired summer generation 6% below the prior summer, while its broader outlook expects record natural-gas consumption in power next year and roughly flat-to-rising gas generation. These are near-term fuel-specific national forecasts, not a five-year constant-quality demand measure for eligible LMM plants. I combine declining coal and resilient gas into a modest fossil-basket decline; the direction can be wrong if electricity-load growth or policy materially changes gas dispatch.
d5.rationale: Coal retirement and renewable additions reduce the basket, but gas remains dispatchable, benefits from load growth and low prices, and can supply capacity when variable resources are unavailable.
d5.change_evidence: EIA Annual Energy Outlook generation by fuel, EIA-860 retirement and addition plans, ISO capacity auctions, data-center load forecasts, and target dispatch curves would materially revise the range.
caveats: Coal and gas trajectories diverge sharply.
Policy, fuel prices, and electricity-load growth are unusually uncertain.

o.rationale: For fossil generation quantity that remains in year five, an accountable licensed and staffed physical plant must operate turbines, boilers, fuel systems, emissions controls, and grid interfaces. Software can automate workflows but cannot supply electricity without the generating asset.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on remotely operated plants, utility insourcing, behind-the-meter self-generation, and autonomous control accepted by regulators and system operators would move the estimate.
caveats: Fuel switching and plant retirement belong mainly in d5.
Operator share may migrate to larger portfolios.

narrative.executive_view: Fossil generation offers a meaningful operational analytics opportunity, but eligibility and benefit retention are constrained by utility ownership, project structures, market pricing, and regulation. Gas demand is resilient while coal decline weakens the combined basket.
narrative.ai_impact: Likely uses include predictive maintenance, alarm and document support, outage planning, heat-rate analysis, fuel and power-market forecasting, environmental reporting, and work management. Control-room accountability, field operations, maintenance, safety response, and compliance sampling remain human-led.
narrative.value_capture: Merchant markets, capacity auctions, tolling, and regulated cost recovery transmit savings to customers or bids. Fixed-price contracts and operational differentiation can preserve a share, but average retention is limited.
narrative.firm_availability: The estimated population overstates actionable supply because many plants sit inside utilities, portfolios, cooperatives, municipalities, or passive project entities. Standalone independent operators with transferable permits, staff, contracts, and interconnection are fewer.
narrative.demand_durability: Coal generation continues to decline, while gas remains a large dispatchable source and can benefit from electricity-load growth. Policy, fuel prices, renewables, storage, and data-center load drive a wide range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Environmental liability, decommissioning, carbon and air rules, fuel and power prices, PPA assignment, capacity-market reform, reliability mandates, cyber risk, project debt, and the mapping from establishments to firms dominate.
narrative.principal_driver: Dispatchable gas generation retains physical value as electricity demand grows and variable resources expand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Coal decline, regulated or competitive pass-through, and non-transferable project ownership sharply reduce the screenable opportunity.

sources.title: Power Plant Operators, Distributors, and Dispatchers
sources.supports: BLS states power-plant operators control systems that generate electricity and operate generating equipment, supporting the physical and accountable work characterization.

sources.title: Power Plant Operators Occupational Employment and Wages
sources.supports: BLS reports 21,990 power-plant operators in electric generation, transmission, and distribution, used as a broader occupation proxy.

sources.title: Electric Utilities Occupational Survey Form
sources.supports: BLS defines power-plant operators as controlling, operating, or maintaining machinery that generates electric power.

sources.title: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook: Electricity, Coal, and Renewables
sources.supports: EIA forecasts summer 2026 coal-fired generation 6% below the prior summer amid increased generation from multiple fuels.

sources.title: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook
sources.supports: EIA expects record natural-gas consumption in the electric-power sector next year, driven by load growth, fleet expansion, and relatively low gas prices.

sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner
sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy.
